target.title: feat(gateway): forward OpenAI stop sequences through chat completions
target.body:
## Summary

- Forward the OpenAI-compatible `stop` request field from inbound `POST /v1/chat/completions` through gateway request parsing, `streamParams`, the embedded runner extra-params layer, and provider transport options.
- Normalize `stop` to a bounded `string[]` at the gateway boundary: accepts a single string or up to four non-empty strings, and returns OpenAI-compatible 400 errors for invalid shapes.
- Send normalized stop sequences to OpenAI-compatible Chat Completions as `stop` and Anthropic Messages as `stop_sequences`; Responses-backed models remain unchanged because the current OpenAI Responses create params do not expose `stop`.
- Document `stop` support in the OpenAI-compatible HTTP gateway docs.

## Verification

- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` — clean
- `pnpm test src/gateway/openai-http.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/extra-params.sampling.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` — gateway shard 18/18, agents shard 267/267
- Local dependency contract check:
  - OpenAI Chat Completions SDK params expose `stop?: string | null | Array<string>`
  - OpenAI Responses SDK params do not expose a `stop` field
  - Anthropic Messages SDK params expose `stop_sequences?: Array<string>`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: OpenAI-compatible Gateway HTTP requests now honor client-supplied `stop` on `POST /v1/chat/completions`; valid stop sequences are forwarded to the upstream OpenAI-compatible provider, and invalid values are rejected at the gateway boundary with deterministic OpenAI-compatible 400 errors.

Real environment tested: Local dev gateway on loopback port 18800, built from rebased commit `779f723455`. Runtime used the local default model `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` through the `openai-completions` adapter. Gateway auth was disabled only in a temporary local proof config; provider credentials stayed in the existing local OpenClaw auth/config storage and were not printed.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started `openclaw gateway run --port 18800 --bind loopback --auth none --allow-unconfigured --compact` with a temporary config enabling `gateway.http.endpoints.chatCompletions.enabled`, waited for `/healthz`, then posted four local requests to `/v1/chat/completions`: one happy-path request with `stop: "STOP"`, one array with five stop entries, one empty-string stop, and one array containing a non-string stop entry.

Evidence after fix:

Happy-path request asked the model to reply exactly `alpha STOP beta` with `stop: "STOP"`:

```json
{
  "httpStatus": 200,
  "finishReason": "stop",
  "contentPreview": "alpha",
  "containsStop": false,
  "containsAfterStopText": false
}
```

Too many stop sequences:

```json
{
  "httpStatus": 400,
  "errorType": "invalid_request_error",
  "message": "Invalid stop: stop supports at most 4 sequences"
}
```

Empty stop string:

```json
{
  "httpStatus": 400,
  "errorType": "invalid_request_error",
  "message": "Invalid stop: stop entries must be non-empty strings"
}
```

Non-string stop array item:

```json
{
  "httpStatus": 400,
  "errorType": "invalid_request_error",
  "message": "Invalid stop: stop entries must be non-empty strings"
}
```

Observed result after fix: The valid request completed through the real local Gateway and upstream OpenAI-compatible provider, stopped before emitting the stop marker or following text, and returned `finish_reason: "stop"`. Invalid stop values were rejected before provider dispatch with stable OpenAI-compatible 400 errors.

What was not tested: Crabbox/Testbox, cross-OS proof, and a live Anthropic provider call were not run. Anthropic transport behavior is covered by the focused unit test for `stop_sequences`. `/v1/responses` was not tested because this PR intentionally leaves Responses unchanged; current OpenAI Responses SDK params do not include a `stop` field.

target.title: fix(discord): deliver same-session channel replies
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Fixes same-session, same-channel Discord guild-channel sessions_send replies being dropped when the async A2A announce decider returns ANNOUNCE_SKIP.
- Keeps true cross-agent announcement routing on the existing announce-decider path.

Why does this matter now?

- Channel-originated sessions can already produce the correct assistant reply, but users never see it when the decider treats it like an agent-to-agent announcement.

What is the intended outcome?

- When a channel session sends to itself from the same channel, the latest assistant reply is delivered directly back to the source channel.
- Cross-session and cross-agent announcements still use the existing decider and ping-pong guard behavior.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No changes to Discord configuration, provider wiring, or broader A2A announcement policy.
- Production Discord guild delivery is still pending reviewer direction before marking the PR ready.

What does success look like?

- A same-session Discord channel reply sends the substantive assistant response instead of returning ANNOUNCE_SKIP.
- Existing cross-agent announce behavior remains unchanged.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether same-session, same-channel source replies are the right boundary for bypassing the announce decider.
- Whether the helper extraction preserves the existing delivery semantics for non-same-session A2A flows.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #87157

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #86723
Related #86725

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- Owner-maintainer follow-up from our investigation of the Discord guild-channel reply path described in #87157.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Same-session Discord guild-channel replies can be generated but skipped by the A2A announce decider before reaching the source channel.
- Real environment tested: pi-claw Linux/aarch64 checkout of this PR branch at 7533df12c2b0cba145fed70ded54eaef3e8a3e93, with dependencies installed from the lockfile.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: From the pi-claw checkout, ran a tsx live harness that imported this branch's runSessionsSendA2AFlow, used a Discord channel session as both targetSessionKey and requesterSessionKey, set requesterChannel to discord, and delivered through a live connected Discord Gateway.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted live output:
  {
    "proofId": "87179-piclaw-1779867020560",
    "head": "7533df12c2b0cba145fed70ded54eaef3e8a3e93",
    "host": "pi-claw",
    "targetSessionKey": "agent:main:discord:channel:[redacted]",
    "requesterSessionKey": "agent:main:discord:channel:[redacted]",
    "requesterChannel": "discord",
    "sendCall": {
      "channel": "discord",
      "to": "channel:[redacted]",
      "accountId": null,
      "threadId": null,
      "message": "PR #87179 live proof 87179-piclaw-1779867020560: same-session Discord reply delivered from pi-claw using patched branch 7533df12.",
      "idempotencyKeyPresent": true
    },
    "gatewayCallMethods": ["send"],
    "sendResult": {
      "runId": "[redacted]",
      "messageId": "[redacted]",
      "channel": "discord",
      "channelId": "[redacted]"
    },
    "sendError": null
  }
- Discord readback after fix: The live channel read returned the same proof text from the bot account at 2026-05-27T07:30:21.436Z, with the returned message id matching the send result (ids redacted here).
- Observed result after fix: The same-session Discord channel flow delivered the substantive reply directly to the source Discord channel. The live harness took the direct send path and did not convert the reply into ANNOUNCE_SKIP.
- What was not tested: A separate lab Discord bot setup on pi-claw still needs guild/intent completion, so the live delivery used the already-connected Discord Gateway.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Discord guild/channel/message ids and internal Gateway URL are redacted from this public PR.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): #87157 reports sessions_send returning accepted/pending while logs show ANNOUNCE_SKIP, leaving the Discord channel without the generated reply.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- git diff --check
- ./node_modules/.bin/tsx -e "import('./src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.ts').then((mod)=>console.log(typeof mod.runSessionsSendA2AFlow))"
- node --import tsx --input-type=module runtime harness for the same-session Discord channel path
- pi-claw live tsx harness from branch 7533df12 invoking runSessionsSendA2AFlow and delivering to a live Discord channel through the connected Gateway
- CI=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 timeout 180s node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts --reporter=verbose src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.test.ts

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added a regression test covering same-session channel replies bypassing the announce decider and delivering the substantive reply directly.
- Focused agents-tools regression file now completes locally and on pi-claw: 14 tests passed in src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.test.ts. This includes the same-session same-channel bypass, same-session different-channel decider path, and cross-session ANNOUNCE_SKIP suppression path.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- In the issue path, the assistant response was generated but the A2A announcement phase returned ANNOUNCE_SKIP, so the Discord channel never received the reply.

If no test was added, why not?

- A focused regression test was added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes/No)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (Yes/No)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (Yes/No)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Accidentally bypassing the announce-decider for flows that are actually cross-agent announcements.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The bypass is limited to requesterSessionKey == targetSessionKey, requesterChannel == announceTarget.channel, and an existing announce target. Cross-session and same-session different-channel flows still use the existing decider path.
- Focused coverage confirms the same-session same-channel bypass while preserving existing announce target behaviors, including same-session different-channel sends using the decider and cross-session ANNOUNCE_SKIP staying silent.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- ClawSweeper and maintainer review can now use the added live Discord proof plus the pi-claw focused regression run.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Waiting on CI/ClawSweeper for the amended head.
- Waiting on maintainer confirmation that same-session, same-channel source replies are the intended boundary for bypassing the A2A announce decider.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed ClawSweeper feedback by tightening the bypass to same-session same-channel replies, completing the focused sessions-send-tool.a2a regression run, adding explicit ANNOUNCE_SKIP suppression coverage, and updating the PR body with that result.
- Added live Discord/running-Gateway proof from pi-claw for the same-session source-channel delivery path.

target.title: feat(feishu): add agent message delete and recall
target.body:
# PR 1 draft: Feishu agent message delete and recall

Title:

```text
feat(feishu): add agent message delete and recall actions
```

## Summary

- Add an agent-callable `feishu_message` tool for `list`, `delete`, `recall`, `read_receipts`, and `read_users`.
- Wire Feishu `messages` tool config through defaults, schema validation, plugin metadata, API exports, and runtime registration.
- Expose Telegram-like generic message actions for Feishu: `delete` and `unsend`.
- Track the latest bot-sent Feishu message per account/chat so natural-language requests like "delete your previous message" can work even when the model omits `messageId`.
- Document Feishu message-management permissions, Unix-second time filters, and delete/recall behavior.

## Related upstream work

- Closes / revives https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/51422
- Supersedes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/52993
- Complements, but does not replace, inbound recall-event work in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/55225 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/55691
- Leaves Feishu reaction tool registration for a follow-up PR, because reaction fixes are already active in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/66478 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77653

## Fixes compared with #52993

- `start_time` and `end_time` are documented and validated as Unix-second timestamp strings, matching Feishu's API, instead of ISO 8601 strings.
- `delete` and `recall` have distinct request paths and user-facing messages; delete no longer reuses recall semantics such as "cannot recall after two minutes".
- Missing Feishu scopes are surfaced with actionable messages, including `im:message.group_msg` for group-message deletion.

## Notes for reviewers

- Feishu delete behaves as a platform soft-delete/recall: follow-up reads can return `deleted: true`, `updated: true`, and empty content. Some clients may still show a deletion placeholder.
- The last-bot-message fallback is intentionally in-memory and scoped by account/chat with a 24-hour TTL. It only records messages sent by this bot process.
- The feature is gated by `tools.messages`, defaulting to `false` because message list/delete/recall/read-receipts are sensitive.

## Testing

```text
corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/message-tool.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.reply-fallback.test.ts
# 4 files, 104 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts
# 2 files, 84 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
# 63 files, 786 tests passed

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions
# passed

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
# passed

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check ...
# passed

corepack pnpm exec oxlint extensions/feishu/src/sent-message-cache.ts extensions/feishu/src/send-target.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
# passed, 0 warnings/errors

git diff --check
# passed
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu agents can delete or recall bot messages from a real group chat through the new `feishu_message` tool and generic `message(action="delete"|"unsend")` path.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 host, local OpenClaw gateway from this branch, enterprise self-built Feishu app using WebSocket mode, real Feishu group chat with the bot added, and Feishu app scopes including `im:message:update`, `im:message:readonly`, and `im:message.group_msg`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enabled the opt-in message management gate for the Feishu test account, started the local gateway from this branch with `openclaw gateway run --force`, sent messages in the real Feishu group, first verified delete failed before `im:message.group_msg` was granted, granted the scope and restarted the gateway, sent a live bot reply, asked the bot to delete its previous message, then checked the deleted message through Feishu message read output.

Evidence after fix: Copied live output and redacted runtime notes from the real Feishu setup:

```text
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
Feishu WebSocket ready at 2026-05-18T15:43:04+08:00

Initial group delete attempt:
  Feishu API error code=230027
  msg="need scope: im:message.group_msg"
  log_id=20260518150333B37818A84E685EE4FE60

After granting im:message.group_msg:
  sent message_id=om_x100b6f9fbdc2c8e4c3bb0f1440e9c1d
  im.message.delete response={"code":0,"msg":"success"}
  follow-up message read:
    deleted=true
    updated=true
    content_length=0

Natural-language group flow after last-bot-message fallback:
  user: delete your previous message
  agent called message(action="delete") without messageId
  fallback resolved last bot message in chat oc_* to om_*
  Feishu delete returned deleted=true
```

Observed result after fix: The bot replied in the Feishu group, deleted its own previous message after the required group-message scope was enabled, and Feishu readback showed the target message was deleted with empty content. The natural-language "delete your previous message" case also worked without the model supplying a `messageId`.

What was not tested: No additional gaps

```text
Natural-language group flow after last-bot-message fallback:
  user: delete your previous message
  agent called message(action="delete") without messageId
  fallback resolved last bot message in chat oc_* to om_*
  Feishu delete returned deleted=true
```

Observed result after fix: The bot replied in the Feishu group, deleted its own previous message after the required group-message scope was enabled, and Feishu readback showed the target message was deleted with empty content. The natural-language "delete your previous message" case also worked without the model supplying a `messageId`.

What was not tested: No additional gaps

target.title: fix(skills): require resolved approval before mutating actions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: several skill docs mention confirmation near mutating commands, but do not always bind approval to the resolved target/content.
- Solution: require preview or resolution first, then approval of the exact action before send/reorder/upload-style commands.
- What changed: tightened the instructions for `ordercli`, `imsg`, `gog`, `wacli`, and `oracle`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no CLI code, command syntax, installer metadata, or changelog changes.

## Motivation

This came from a research pass over skill semantics around real side effects.

One concrete case is `ordercli`. Before this patch, the doc listed:

```bash
ordercli foodora reorder <orderCode> --confirm --address-id <id>
```

The missing guard was not the command itself; it was the approval object. A plausible flow is:

1. User says: "reorder my last sushi order, use the office address."
2. The agent resolves an order code and address ID.
3. The agent runs the `--confirm` command without first showing the resolved items, fees, substitutions, and address.

This patch makes the approval target explicit: preview or resolve first, show the concrete target/content, then run the mutating command only after that preview is approved.

The same pattern applies to messaging and mail. "Text Alex that I'm running late" should not send until `Alex` has been resolved to a specific phone/chat and the final body is shown back.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: skill instructions now require a resolved preview before high-impact actions such as `ordercli ... --confirm`, `imsg send`, `gog gmail send` / calendar writes, `wacli send`, and Oracle uploads.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `skill-safety-gates`, commit `dd2c700ea0`, based on `origin/main`. I used the isolated dev profile so the proof did not depend on my normal `~/.openclaw` config.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm openclaw --dev skills info ordercli --json
pnpm openclaw --dev skills info imsg --json
node --import tsx
# loaded `loadWorkspaceSkillEntries()` from src/agents/skills/workspace.ts
# against a temporary workspace and bundledSkillsDir=./skills

# with temporary command shims in /tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:
OPENCLAW_PROOF_LOG_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof PATH="/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:$PATH" imsg chats --limit 20 --json
OPENCLAW_PROOF_LOG_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof PATH="/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:$PATH" ordercli foodora history --limit 20
OPENCLAW_PROOF_LOG_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof PATH="/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:$PATH" ordercli foodora reorder FDO-2468
OPENCLAW_PROOF_LOG_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof PATH="/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:$PATH" wacli chats list --limit 20 --query Alex
OPENCLAW_PROOF_LOG_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof PATH="/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:$PATH" wacli messages search yesterday --limit 20 --chat 15559876543@s.whatsapp.net
cat /tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/commands.log
cat /tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/mutations.log
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ pnpm openclaw --dev skills info ordercli --json
{
  "name": "ordercli",
  "source": "openclaw-bundled",
  "filePath": "/Users/hanzhi/projects/ahl-popl-upstream-prs/openclaw/skills/ordercli/SKILL.md",
  "modelVisible": true,
  "userInvocable": true,
  "commandVisible": true,
  "missing": { "bins": [], "anyBins": [], "env": [], "config": [], "os": [] }
}

$ pnpm openclaw --dev skills info imsg --json
{
  "name": "imsg",
  "source": "openclaw-bundled",
  "filePath": "/Users/hanzhi/projects/ahl-popl-upstream-prs/openclaw/skills/imsg/SKILL.md",
  "modelVisible": true,
  "userInvocable": true,
  "commandVisible": true,
  "missing": { "bins": [], "anyBins": [], "env": [], "config": [], "os": [] }
}

$ node --import tsx
LOADED ordercli openclaw-bundled /Users/hanzhi/projects/ahl-popl-upstream-prs/openclaw/skills/ordercli/SKILL.md modelVisible true
ordercli:55: - Confirm after showing restaurant, items, address, fees, and total:
ordercli:59: - Never treat `--confirm` as user consent. Run the preview first and wait for
LOADED imsg openclaw-bundled /Users/hanzhi/projects/ahl-popl-upstream-prs/openclaw/skills/imsg/SKILL.md modelVisible true
imsg:104: 1. **Resolve first, send second** - show phone/chat ID, service, text, and attachments
imsg:105: 2. **Wait for explicit approval of the shown preview** before `imsg send`
imsg:119: # "Found Mom at +1555123456. Send 'I'll be late' via iMessage?"

Loaded SKILL.md: skills/imsg/SKILL.md
Task: Text Mom that I'll be late.
Read-only lookup: imsg chats --limit 20 --json
Resolved: Mom, +1555123456, iMessage
Prompt before send: Found Mom at +1555123456. Send 'I'll be late' via iMessage?

Loaded SKILL.md: skills/ordercli/SKILL.md
Task: Reorder my last Foodora order.
Preview: ordercli foodora reorder FDO-2468
Resolved preview: Noodle House, Dan dan noodles + Cucumber salad, 1 Market St, fees 2.49 EUR, total 24.80 EUR, cartChanged=false
Prompt before cart change: Reorder FDO-2468 from Noodle House to 1 Market St for 24.80 EUR, including fees?

Loaded SKILL.md: skills/wacli/SKILL.md
Task: WhatsApp Alex that I will send the agenda shortly.
Read-only lookup: wacli chats list --limit 20 --query Alex
Resolved: Alex Chen, 15559876543@s.whatsapp.net, +15559876543
Prompt before send: Send WhatsApp message to Alex Chen at 15559876543@s.whatsapp.net: "I will send the agenda shortly"?

Command log:
imsg chats --limit 20 --json
ordercli foodora history --limit 20
ordercli foodora reorder FDO-2468
wacli chats list --limit 20 --query Alex
wacli messages search yesterday --limit 20 --chat 15559876543@s.whatsapp.net

Mutation log:
<empty>

PASS: no `imsg send`, `ordercli ... --confirm`, or `wacli send` command was executed.
```

Observed result after fix: a patched OpenClaw dev setup exposes the affected bundled skills as model-visible/user-invocable skills, and the loaded skill content contains the resolved-preview approval guidance. The behavior rehearsal stopped at the confirmation prompts and did not execute the mutating commands.

What was not tested: unrelated OpenClaw runtime internals. This branch proof used temporary shims instead of mutating real accounts/orders; the observed behavior is that the rehearsed flows stopped at the resolved-preview confirmation before those commands.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the docs used "confirm" language without always defining what exact resolved object must be confirmed.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no wording that separated the user's initial request from approval of a later resolved recipient/order/file set.
- Contributing context (if known): these skills wrap real-world integrations where a natural-language target can resolve to concrete IDs only after lookup.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A for this docs-only change.
- Scenario the test should lock in: future skill text should keep approval attached to the resolved target/content for mutating actions.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is currently governed by skill instructions, so prose review plus diff checks are the direct guardrail.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none found.
- If no new test is added, why not: no runtime code path changed.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Agents following these skills will ask for approval after showing the resolved target/content before sends, reorders, calendar writes, or context uploads.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[user request] -> [agent resolves target] -> [mutating command]

After:
[user request] -> [agent resolves target] -> [preview shown] -> [approval] -> [mutating command]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Yes
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `oracle` now explicitly requires dry-run file review and redaction before sharing sensitive files. This narrows, rather than expands, secret exposure.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local git checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): skill docs for Foodora, iMessage, Google Workspace, WhatsApp, and Oracle
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Review the five changed `SKILL.md` files.
2. Check that each mutating/send/upload path requires preview or resolution before approval.
3. Run `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`.

### Expected

- No whitespace errors.
- No runtime code changes.
- Skill docs bind approval to resolved target/content.

### Actual

- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD` produced no errors.
- Only the five intended skill docs changed.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Snippet:

```text
dd2c700ea0 fix(skills): require resolved approval before mutating actions
5 files changed, 20 insertions(+), 11 deletions(-)
```

## Human Verification (required)

What was manually verified:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `ordercli`: reorder flow now requires preview approval before `--confirm`.
  - `imsg`: send flow now requires resolved phone/chat, service, body, and attachment preview.
  - `gog`: `--no-input` is limited to read-only scripting, and mail/calendar writes require preview.
  - `wacli`: send flow now requires exact destination/content approval.
  - `oracle`: file sharing now requires dry-run file review and redaction for sensitive files.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Address override for reorders.
  - Unknown or ambiguous message recipients.
  - Calendar event creation.
  - Sensitive file patterns in Oracle context selection.
## Review Conversations

- [x] No bot review conversations exist yet.
- [x] No addressed bot review conversations are being left unresolved.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: workflows that used to proceed immediately after a broad request may now require one more user round-trip.
  - Mitigation: the extra approval is limited to mutating/send/upload actions; read-only commands are unchanged.
- Risk: this is instruction-level protection, not CLI-level enforcement. Agents that ignore the skill text can still call the underlying commands directly.
  - Mitigation: the affected docs now put the guard next to the commands reviewers and agents are most likely to follow. CLI-level enforcement can be added separately if maintainers want stronger guarantees.
- Risk: preview details differ by integration and CLI output, so a fixed checklist could become stale.
  - Mitigation: the wording asks for the resolved target/content and names the important fields without changing CLI output schemas.

target.title: Preserve agent hard timeout attribution
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #87444 by preserving the distinction between a real run timeout and ordinary abort/cancel/error signals after an agent run crosses its configured hard deadline.

The change carries hard-timeout attribution through the Gateway wait/dedupe paths, subagent lifecycle reconciliation, task registry state, SDK normalization, OpenResponses projection, ACP relay, TUI projection, and persisted session recovery. It also keeps non-timeout aborts distinct, so user/RPC cancellation still normalizes as cancelled instead of timed out.

Related coverage: this also partially addresses #76962 by ensuring a terminal subagent timeout announcement is no longer held behind a still-active embedded child run. It does not claim to fully fix #76962's physical `claude -p` process cleanup, exec approval queue cancellation, or late direct-output leak; those remain separate proof/fix surfaces.

LoC churn: application code `+1303/-230`; non-application code, including tests and test helpers, `+4827/-337`.

## Why This Shape

The reported failure was one symptom of a broader invariant: once a run has hit its hard timeout, later cleanup signals must not erase or reclassify that timeout. Several surfaces observe the same run at different times, so the fix is intentionally cross-path rather than limited to the original `agent.wait` symptom.

The highest-risk sibling behavior is cancellation. This PR adds coverage that cancelled/RPC-stopped runs remain cancelled while hard-timeout runs remain timed out.

The #76962 overlap is intentionally narrow: terminal timeout announcement now proceeds from the already-final timeout outcome instead of waiting for the embedded child process to settle. That removes one path where timeout delivery could be delayed by a still-active child, while leaving process termination and late-output suppression to the dedicated zombie-process cleanup path.

## Verification

- `$autoreview`: clean, zero accepted/actionable findings.
- `git diff --check`: clean.
- Targeted regression suite on AWS Crabbox `run_3eff1d4287bd`: 31 files passed, 153 tests passed, 909 skipped.
- Live Gateway/API matrix on AWS Crabbox `run_3eff1d4287bd`: all 8 release-candidate scenarios passed.
- Package/channel proof on AWS Crabbox `run_3eff1d4287bd`: built an npm tarball from this branch, installed that package in a Docker environment, and passed the Telegram round-trip channel test using a Convex-leased `telegram` credential.
- Related issue probe on AWS Crabbox `run_68e8283ae722`: latest `origin/main` reproduced the #76962 sub-symptom by calling the embedded-child wait on a terminal timeout; this branch passed the same before/after probe by announcing without that wait.

The live matrix covered:

- The reported subagent hard-timeout path.
- Fresh `agent.wait` and task terminal state.
- SDK `runs.create`, `run.wait`, and cancellation normalization.
- OpenResponses non-streaming and streaming responses.
- Persisted session reconstruction after terminal timeout state is written.
- Package-installed channel delivery through Telegram.
- The #76962-related terminal-timeout announcement path that must not wait behind an active child run.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Subagent hard run timeouts now remain terminal timeouts across Gateway wait/dedupe, task registry, SDK, OpenResponses, persisted session reconstruction, and channel delivery, instead of being lost or misclassified after late abort/rejection/completion signals. The branch also prevents terminal timeout announcements from waiting behind an active embedded child run, partially addressing #76962.

Real environment tested: AWS Crabbox Linux `c7a.8xlarge`, provider `aws`, lease `cbx_ab678695114e`, slug `harbor-crab`, run `run_3eff1d4287bd`; related #76962 probe also ran on AWS Crabbox `run_68e8283ae722`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran autoreview, then ran a release-candidate proof matrix on AWS Crabbox against this branch. The matrix started a real Gateway with live OpenAI model access, exercised the reported subagent timeout behavior and sibling API paths, built the branch into an npm package tarball, installed that tarball in Docker, and verified Telegram channel round-trip delivery. A separate before/after Crabbox probe compared latest `origin/main` with this branch for the #76962 terminal-timeout announcement sub-symptom.

Evidence after fix: The targeted regression suite passed; the live Gateway/API matrix passed all 8 scenarios; the package-installed Telegram Docker E2E passed; and the #76962-related probe passed on this branch after reproducing on latest `origin/main`.

Observed result after fix: The reported path returned a terminal timeout with no active child runs remaining, delivered completion state, `agent.wait` status `timeout`, and timeout outcome preserved after late aborted/rejection signals. For the #76962-related probe, terminal timeout announcement proceeded without waiting for the still-active embedded child run.

What was not tested: The final live matrix used `openai/gpt-5.4-mini` because earlier `gpt-5.5` attempts hit OpenAI TPM limits. The #76962 probe did not prove physical `claude -p` process termination, exec approval queue cancellation, or late direct-output suppression.

target.title: Harden SSRF reserved-host IDNA checks [AI]
target.body:
## Summary

- Behavior addressed: block IDNA/punycode A-label encodings of reserved SSRF hostnames before DNS resolution or fetch dispatch.
- Root cause: the guard compared the URL parser's ASCII hostname directly against reserved names/suffixes, so fake A-labels such as `xn--localhost-` and `svc.xn--internal-` did not match even though their Unicode-decoded form is `localhost` / `svc.internal`.
- Scope boundary: this only adds an IDNA-decoded reserved-host comparison; it does not change general hostname normalization, configured local-origin trust, DNS pinning, or private-IP policy behavior.
- Intended outcome: exact reserved hostnames and reserved suffixes stay blocked across ASCII, repeated-dot, Unicode-dot, percent-decoded, and IDNA A-label spellings.
- Compatibility note: helpers that intentionally classify private-network targets now treat A-label spellings of reserved local names the same way they already treat the ASCII reserved names.

AI-assisted.

What should reviewers focus on?

- `src/infra/net/ssrf.ts` now keeps the existing normalized/IP checks and adds a decoded-hostname comparison only for reserved-host classification.
- `src/infra/net/ssrf.test.ts` and `src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ssrf.test.ts` cover both direct helper behavior and the full guarded fetch path.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Does not close an issue.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #87305.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No maintainer request; adjacent hardening after #87305.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:

Reserved-host SSRF checks missed fake A-label encodings whose IDNA-decoded form is an existing blocked hostname/suffix.

- Real environment tested:

Linux, Node v22.22.1, branch head `091bfc90336a3159a46d8f3113bea9bd6804cf5c` rebased onto `upstream/main` `2e042fbca8fa4a42df709acfe3cb27f68b79a7e8`.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'PROBE'
// Imports src/infra/net/ssrf.ts and src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ts,
// then calls isBlockedHostnameOrIp, assertHostnameAllowedWithPolicy,
// and fetchWithSsrFGuard with fake public DNS plus a fetch spy.
PROBE
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

Terminal capture from this branch, copied live output:

```json
{
  "node": "v22.22.1",
  "head": "091bfc90336a3159a46d8f3113bea9bd6804cf5c",
  "base": "2e042fbca8fa4a42df709acfe3cb27f68b79a7e8",
  "mergeBase": "2e042fbca8fa4a42df709acfe3cb27f68b79a7e8",
  "rows": [
    {
      "host": "xn--localhost-",
      "unicode": "localhost",
      "parsedHostname": "xn--localhost-",
      "blocked": true,
      "fetchGuard": {
        "outcome": "blocked-or-error",
        "fetchCalls": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "host": "api.xn--localhost-",
      "unicode": "api.localhost",
      "parsedHostname": "api.xn--localhost-",
      "blocked": true,
      "fetchGuard": {
        "outcome": "blocked-or-error",
        "fetchCalls": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "host": "svc.xn--local-",
      "unicode": "svc.local",
      "parsedHostname": "svc.xn--local-",
      "blocked": true,

      }
    },
    {
      "host": "svc.xn--internal-",

      }

        "outcome": "blocked-or-error",
        "fetchCalls": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "host": "metadata.google.xn--internl-7fg",
      "unicode": "metadata.google.internаl",
      "parsedHostname": "metadata.google.xn--internl-7fg",
      "blocked": false,
      "fetchGuard": {

```

- Observed result after fix:

A-labels that decode to the existing reserved names/suffixes are blocked before fetch (`fetchCalls: 0`). The non-reserved Cyrillic homograph control still reaches the fake public fetch path, showing this is not a broad confusable-name block.

- What was not tested:

No live network call was made; the runtime proof uses the production guard path with fake public DNS and a fetch spy so the evidence can distinguish blocked-before-fetch from reached-fetch without contacting external hosts.

- Proof limitations or environment constraints:

This proof covers default reserved-host blocking. It does not change the intentional configured local-origin policy that can allow exact local provider origins.

- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

The same probe on current main before the patch showed `xn--localhost-`, `api.xn--localhost-`, `svc.xn--local-`, `svc.xn--internal-`, and `metadata.google.xn--internal-` all had `blocked: false` and `fetchGuard.outcome: "reached-fetch"` with `fetchCalls: 1`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/net/ssrf.test.ts src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ssrf.test.ts src/infra/net/hostname.test.ts
# 3 files / 157 tests passed

git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD
pnpm format:check src/infra/net/ssrf.ts src/infra/net/ssrf.test.ts src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ssrf.test.ts
# All matched files use the correct format.
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
# autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Direct SSRF helper coverage for fake A-label reserved names/suffixes.
- Guarded fetch coverage proving those URLs are rejected before fetch is called.
- A homograph non-match control to keep the patch limited to decoded reserved ASCII names/suffixes.

What failed before this fix, if known?

The direct probe showed the fake A-label reserved-host cases reached the fake fetch path before the patch.

If no test was added, why not?

Regression tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. URLs with A-label encodings that decode to the existing reserved SSRF hostnames/suffixes are now rejected.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. This tightens SSRF hostname classification before DNS/fetch. It also aligns intentional private-network target classification so A-label encodings of reserved local names behave like their decoded ASCII reserved names.

What is the highest-risk area?

False positives for ordinary IDN hostnames, and reviewers noticing the intentional alignment for private-network target classification.

How is that risk mitigated?

The decoded comparison reuses the existing reserved-host predicate only. A homograph control (`metadata.google.xn--internl-7fg`, decoding to a Cyrillic-`a` `internаl`) remains allowed unless the existing DNS/IP checks reject its resolved address. Bare exact `internal` / `local` behavior is intentionally unchanged here and can be evaluated separately if OpenClaw wants those labels to become pre-DNS blocks too.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after publication.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Waiting on maintainer review and CI after publication. Local validation was run after rebasing onto `upstream/main` `2e042fbca8fa4a42df709acfe3cb27f68b79a7e8`; final pre-public fresh-base check against latest `upstream/main` `8e56c024df193c0756969575544373b8b961359f` found no overlap with the three PR files and a clean merge-tree (`3dade995da02e9a08808762957e079a29a4218de`). Latest main CI was `in_progress` at run `26622335535` (https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26622335535) at the final pre-public check.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Local autoreview initially flagged a direct changelog edit; the changelog line was removed and the final autoreview pass was clean.

target.title: fix(telegram): expose spooled handler timeout config
target.body:
## Summary
- expose `channels.telegram.spooledUpdateHandlerTimeoutMs` with top-level and per-account config support
- pass the configured value into Telegram isolated polling sessions
- document the timeout and regenerate bundled channel config metadata

## Why
Telegram isolated ingress already fails stale claimed spool updates after a handler timeout, but operators could not tune that timeout from `channels.telegram`. A wedged `.processing` file can therefore keep a same-chat lane blocked until the default 25 minute timeout.

## Runtime Proof
I ran a redacted local runtime harness against the PR head using the real `TelegramPollingSession`, real spool files, real reply-fence timeout recovery path, and a fake Telegram Bot runtime to avoid live network calls. The harness configured `isolatedIngress.spooledUpdateHandlerTimeoutMs = 200`, wrote two same-lane spooled updates, wedged update `42`, and verified update `43` drained only after timeout recovery restarted isolated ingress.

```json
{
  "configuredTimeoutMs": 200,
  "configuredAbortGraceMs": 200,
  "elapsedMs": 475,
  "workerCreateCount": 2,
  "botConstructCount": 2,
  "events": [
    "first:42",
    "second:43"
  ],
  "failedUpdateIds": [
    42
  ],
  "pendingUpdateIds": [],
  "timeoutLog": "[telegram] Telegram isolated polling spool handler timed out behind update 42 on lane telegram:-100:topic:10 after 205ms; marking the update failed, aborting active reply work, and restarting isolated ingress so later updates can drain."
}
```

This shows the configured timeout was honored by the isolated-ingress runtime path: update `42` was failed after the configured timeout budget and the later same-lane update `43` drained after the restart.

## Validation
- `pnpm config:channels:check`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts` passed locally while preparing the patch. A later rerun in this workspace timed out in Vitest/Rolldown pre-test bundling with repeated plugin timing warnings before any assertion output.

target.title: Add plugin manifest contract for SecretRef provider integrations
target.body:
## Summary
- add a manifest-level SecretRef provider integration contract for plugins
- store plugin-owned provider refs in `secrets.providers.<alias>.pluginIntegration` instead of copying plugin resolver `command`/`args` into config
- resolve the current exec command from the active plugin manifest at startup/reload and SecretRef resolution time
- expose SDK types and cover manifest normalization/materialization with focused tests

## Why
External providers like Vault, OneCLI, 1Password, Bitwarden, and similar stores were already possible through manual exec SecretRef configuration. This makes them discoverable, portable, and installable as plugins without hardcoding provider-specific logic in OpenClaw core.

## Real Behavior Proof
Behavior addressed: External SecretRef providers could already be wired manually with `exec`, but plugin-owned Vault-style integrations were not discoverable or portable through plugin manifest metadata. This patch lets config refer to the owning plugin integration and lets OpenClaw materialize the resolver from the installed plugin manifest.

Real environment tested: OpenShift namespace `openclaw-bob`, running pod `openclaw-cbbc765d8-sth7w`, container `gateway`; git-installed Vault plugin from `git:github.com/sallyom/claw-vault`; Vault available at `http://vault.vault.svc:8200`; OpenClaw image from this PR branch. Earlier proof also covered a local Podman OpenClaw + HashiCorp Vault dev setup using an external git-installed Vault plugin and OpenRouter.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
openclaw vault -h
openclaw vault status --json
```
The running deployment also confirmed configured models are accessible using Vault-resolved SecretRefs.

Evidence after fix:
```text
Usage: openclaw vault [options] [command]

Manage Vault SecretRefs

Commands:
  setup       Create a Vault SecretRef setup plan
  status      Show Vault SecretRef provider status
```

```json
{
  "providerAlias": "vault",
  "provider": {
    "configured": true,
    "source": "exec",
    "pluginIntegration": {
      "pluginId": "vault",
      "integrationId": "vault"
    }
  },
  "resolverScript": "/home/node/.openclaw/git/git-c317c86c2672b3ca/repo/dist/vault-secret-ref-resolver.js",
  "vaultAddr": "http://vault.vault.svc:8200",
  "kvMount": "secret",
  "kvVersion": "2",
  "hasVaultToken": true
}
```
Prior live proof also ran `openclaw secrets audit --allow-exec --json` and a live OpenRouter agent turn using a Vault-resolved model API key; terminal-command repro is captured at https://gist.github.com/sallyom/50f4678b053eaa93c4311a5509e52aeb.

Observed result after fix: The plugin-owned `openclaw vault` CLI command is registered, `openclaw vault status --json` reports the new `pluginIntegration` contract with canonical plugin id `vault`, the resolver script is loaded from the installed plugin root, Vault runtime env is visible without printing the token, and configured models are accessible through Vault-resolved SecretRefs.

What was not tested: Production Vault auth methods beyond the current deployment setup, and non-Vault providers such as 1Password, Bitwarden, or OneCLI.

Before evidence: Before this contract, the same Vault resolver could only be wired by hand through explicit `secrets.providers.vault.command` / `args` config; there was no plugin manifest preset for OpenClaw to discover or materialize.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/secrets/provider-integrations.test.ts src/secrets/resolve.test.ts src/secrets/plan.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`

target.title: feat(memory): add Memory Audit review queue
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Adds Memory Audit as a human-reviewed memory quality pass for cleanup, correction, and promotion from daily/session evidence into durable targets.
- Exposes audit collection/staging tools, Gateway doctor methods, and a dashboard Audit tab with review and settings flows.
- Adds managed daily/weekly audit cron reconciliation while keeping Memory Audit separate from Dreaming promotion.

Why does this matter now?

- Dreaming is useful as an automated promotion loop, but in practice it can promote low-value or stale fragments into durable memory, and it does not put those writes through a human review queue.
- Memory Audit is the safer alternative for memory quality work: it uses an LLM to inspect writable memory targets plus daily memory and session-log evidence, stage add/edit/delete/move recommendations, and leave the final apply/reject decision to a human.
- The current PR targets `AGENTS.md`, `MEMORY.md`, `USER.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `shared-memory.md`, while treating `memory/*.md` daily files and session logs as read-only evidence for promotion.

What is the intended outcome?

- Operators can enable Memory Audit, review staged recommendations, apply/reject items, and let managed cron jobs collect/stage future suggestions from daily memories and session logs into the right writable target.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Replacing or removing Dreaming.
- Mutating raw session logs or historical daily memory files during apply.
- Adding `IDENTITY.md` and `SOUL.md` as a targets in this PR.
- Live production rollout proof beyond local Gateway/config/test validation.

What does success look like?

- Memory Audit settings and review UI work through the dashboard.
- The owning memory plugin is enabled when Memory Audit is enabled.
- Managed audit cron jobs reconcile reliably after Gateway restart/startup.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Config/restart behavior for `plugins.entries.memory-core.config.memoryAudit`.
- Gateway doctor method scope and handler registration.
- Memory Audit apply/reject behavior and file-surface safety.
- Dashboard settings/review UX and cron reconciliation behavior.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

- None.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

- None.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No explicit linked issue in this PR body.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Memory Audit adds a human-reviewed memory cleanup queue, audits daily memory and session-log evidence, stages promotion into `AGENTS.md`/`MEMORY.md`/`USER.md`/`TOOLS.md`/shared memory, and fixes enablement so enabling audit also enables the owning memory plugin and requests a safe Gateway restart for schedule reconciliation.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout in the `feature/memory-audit` worktree after rebasing on latest `upstream/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/memory-audit.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
  - `pnpm ui:i18n:sync`
  - `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.ts ui/src/i18n/locales/*.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.ts ui/src/ui/views/memory-audit.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/format-docs.mjs docs/concepts/memory-audit.md`
  - `git diff --check`
  - Scope check: `git diff --name-only upstream/main...HEAD | rg '^(src/tasks/|extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager)' || true`
- Evidence after fix: all targeted passing commands above passed locally; the scope check returned no task-flow or QMD mcporter files.
- Observed result after fix: Memory Audit tests cover `AGENTS.md`, daily memory, session-log evidence, read-only evidence safety, and promotion into writable targets; UI tests cover new kind labels; cron tests cover the updated audit prompt and startup retry reconciliation; gateway reload tests preserve the Memory Audit restart-required contract.
- What was not tested: full end-to-end dashboard interaction in a browser and a live Gateway restart that creates persistent audit cron jobs from a real operator click. `src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts` was attempted but could not import in this worktree because `src/gateway/protocol` is absent.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: local tests and source-level validation only; no screenshots or remote/live artifact attached.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): local diagnosis showed `memoryAudit.enabled=true` while `plugins.entries.memory-core.enabled=false`, with no Memory Audit cron jobs present.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/memory-audit.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:sync`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Memory Audit review queue, apply/reject behavior, `AGENTS.md` target handling, daily memory/session-log evidence collection, read-only source safety, Gateway doctor payload typing, dashboard controller/view coverage, managed cron reconciliation, i18n output, and config reload planning.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Enabling Memory Audit could leave `memory-core` disabled, so the dashboard looked enabled while plugin hooks/tools and managed cron jobs were unavailable.
- Audit cron reconciliation only ran at `gateway_start` and could miss cron service availability during startup ordering.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added and updated.

Yes.

Yes.

- Memory Audit config changes retain the restart-required contract and managed cron reconciliation has focused coverage.

target.title: Feat/browser screenshot vision
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: When the main model is text-only (e.g. `tencent-tokenhub/hy3-preview`), browser screenshots return image content blocks that the model cannot read — the agent sees `[image omitted]` and cannot reason about the page.
- Why it matters: Browser-use workflows are unusable with text-only main models; operators must switch to an expensive multimodal main model just to get screenshot readback.
- What changed:
  - The browser screenshot action now optionally runs the captured image through a configured vision model and returns a text description instead of an image block. This is controlled by new fields on the top-level `browser` config namespace (`models`, `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, etc.). When unconfigured, behavior is unchanged.
  - Vision descriptions are wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` (same as snapshot/tabs results) to mitigate prompt injection from untrusted web page content.
  - Local screenshot file size is checked against `maxBytes` before sending to the vision provider.
  - `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` in `src/media-understanding/runtime-types.ts` gains `profile`/`preferredProfile` fields, forwarded through `describeImageFileWithModel` to `describeImage` — enabling auth profile selection for all callers of this API, not just the browser tool.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the agent loop, replay-history, session persistence, transport layer, or any other tool. The vision call is entirely contained within the browser extension's screenshot action. The existing `tools.media.image` config and `image_describe` tool are untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: browser-use with text-only models
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts` (20 tests)
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `isBrowserVisionEnabled` returns true/false based on config presence, `enabled: false`, empty models, missing provider/model, CLI-type entries
  - `describeBrowserImageWithVision` calls through to `describeImageFileWithModel` with correct params including prompt, timeout, profile, preferredProfile
  - Local file paths as `mediaUrl` are not forwarded (only HTTP(S) URLs)
  - HTTP(S) `mediaUrl` is correctly forwarded
  - Fallback to next candidate when primary fails
  - Empty model response treated as failure and falls through
  - All candidates failing produces aggregate error with per-candidate details
  - `maxChars` truncation with `[truncated]` marker
  - `maxBytes` enforcement via file stat before vision call
  - `profile`/`preferredProfile` forwarded to `describeImageFileWithModel`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The helper is a pure function with injected deps — unit tests cover all branches without needing a real browser or vision API.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `browser-tool.test.ts` mock infrastructure extended to include `describeImageFileWithModel` dep injection.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config fields on the top-level `browser` namespace for vision model configuration:
  ```json
  {
    "browser": {
      "models": [{ "provider": "bytedance", "model": "doubao-seed-2.0-pro" }]
    }
  }
  ```
  Supported fields: `models[]` (with `provider`, `model`, `prompt`, `maxChars`, `maxBytes`, `timeoutSeconds`, `profile`, `preferredProfile`), `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, `visionMaxChars`, `visionMaxBytes`, `visionTimeoutSeconds`.
- When `browser.models` contains at least one valid provider/model entry and `visionEnabled` is not `false`, every browser screenshot is automatically described by the configured vision model. The tool returns a text content block (`[analyzed by <provider>/<model>]\n<wrapped description>`) instead of an image content block. The description text is wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` to guard against prompt injection.
- When `browser.models` is **not** configured, behavior is identical to before — screenshots return image content blocks.
- Set `visionEnabled: false` to force-disable vision without removing the model list.
- If vision description fails (API error, timeout, empty response, file exceeds `maxBytes`), the tool falls back to returning the original image block plus a warning text block so the agent loop can still recover.
- `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` now accepts optional `profile`/`preferredProfile` fields. This is a backward-compatible additive change to the media-understanding runtime contract. All existing callers are unaffected; new callers (like browser vision) can pass auth profile selection.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before (text-only main model):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool returns image content block
    -> transport replaces with "[image omitted]"
    -> agent cannot reason about page

After (browser.models configured):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool captures screenshot to disk
    -> stat file size → check maxBytes
    -> tool calls vision model (describeImageFileWithModel)
    -> wrapExternalContent(description)
    -> tool returns wrapped text description
    -> text-only main model reads description normally

After (browser.models NOT configured):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool returns image content block (unchanged)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No — reuses existing `describeImageFileWithModel` which handles auth through the standard provider registry, SecretRef mechanism, and (now) optional auth profiles.
- New/changed network calls? Yes — when `browser.models` is configured, the browser tool makes an additional API call to the configured vision provider to describe the screenshot.
  - Risk: The screenshot image is sent to the configured vision provider's API endpoint.
  - Mitigation: This is opt-in (only when operator explicitly configures `browser.models`). The image data flow is identical to `tools.media.image` / `image_describe` tool — same `describeImageFileWithModel` pipeline, same auth, same SSRF guards.
  - Risk: Vision model descriptions contain untrusted web page content that could include prompt injection.
  - Mitigation: All vision description text is wrapped with `wrapExternalContent(text, { source: "browser", includeWarning: true })`, matching the security treatment applied to snapshot and tabs results.
  - Risk: Raw screenshot could be auto-delivered to chat channels if exposed as deliverable media (related: #44759).
  - Mitigation: Addressed — the vision-success path does not include any `MEDIA:` directive or `details.media` in the tool result. The raw screenshot file remains on disk for diagnostic purposes only but is not advertised as deliverable media, preventing auto-send of sensitive page content to chat channels.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (darwin)
- Runtime: Node 22, OpenClaw dev build from source (npm link)
- Model/provider: Main model `tencent-tokenhub/hy3-preview` (text-only); vision model `bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`
- Integration/channel: Control UI webchat
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "browser": {
      "models": [{ "provider": "bytedance", "model": "doubao-seed-2.0-pro" }]
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure `browser.models` with a vision-capable provider/model
2. Start gateway, open dashboard
3. In a new session, ask the agent to take a browser screenshot (e.g. "open https://example.com and take a screenshot")

### Expected

- Agent receives a text description of the screenshot instead of an image block
- Text-only main model can read and reason about the page content
- Tool result includes `[analyzed by <provider>/<model>]` attribution
- Description text is wrapped in external content markers

### Actual

- Confirmed working: agent received text description, main model reasoned about page content normally

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets: Gateway log shows `[browser/vision] described screenshot via bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro (chars=NNN)` on successful vision call
- [x] Failing before + passing after: Before config, agent sees `[image omitted]`; after config, agent reads full page description
- [x] Unit tests: 20 tests passing in `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts`
- [x] CI: `pnpm check:changed` passes (typecheck core/extensions/tests + lint core/extensions + all guards)
- [x] Screenshot/recording

### Video: Vision model describes screenshot for text-only agent

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/32826a25-ca02-4188-9bee-cd7e4bec6639

### Video: Fallback sanitization (new, current HEAD)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d7051b53-6935-4f8c-8899-b60fc55ce72d

Source-level evidence for the image content block that this fallback path also returns (the binary `image` content block itself is not easy to film in webchat, so it is shown via the gateway log line emitted by `imageResultFromFile` → `resolveRuntimeImageSanitization` → sharp resize):

```
2026-05-21T09:11:23.579+08:00 [browser/vision] candidate fail-on-purpose-for-fallback-demo/no-such-model failed: image description timed out after 2000ms
2026-05-21T09:11:23.614+08:00 [agents/tool-images] Image resized to fit limits: 2000x1140px 74.8KB -> 9.2KB (-87.7%)
```

Interpretation:
- Line 1 = the vision provider call rejected/timed out, so the screenshot tool entered the `catch` fallback at `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts:833`.
- Line 2 = the fallback called `imageResultFromFile({ ..., imageSanitization: resolveRuntimeImageSanitization() })` with `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx: 512`, and the upstream image-result helper resized the raw 2000×1140 screenshot before the image content block was added to the tool result. Without the P2 fix, this `imageSanitization` argument was absent, so the original 2000×1140 image would have been returned unscaled.

Reproduction config used for the video:

```jsonc
{
  "agents": { "defaults": { "imageMaxDimensionPx": 512 } },
  "browser": {
    "models": [
      { "provider": "fail-on-purpose-for-fallback-demo", "model": "no-such-model", "timeoutSeconds": 2 }
    ]
  }
}
```

A non-existent vision provider id is used so the failure is deterministic and does not depend on any external network call. `visionEnabled` defaults to enabled whenever `browser.models[]` resolves at least one valid candidate, and `visionTimeoutSeconds` is only consulted as the fallback when an individual `models[].timeoutSeconds` is unset, so both top-level fields are omitted here.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Text-only main models can now read and reason about browser screenshots via a configured vision model, instead of receiving `[image omitted]`.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.3 arm64, Node v25.8.1, OpenClaw built from source (`pnpm build` → `node dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`), main model hy3-preview (tencent-tokenhub, text-only), vision model doubao-seed-2.0-pro (bytedance), webchat dashboard.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Built source: `pnpm build`
  2. Started gateway from source: `node dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`
  3. Configured `browser.models` with `bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`
  4. Opened webchat dashboard, asked agent to screenshot a Discord page
  5. Agent captured screenshot → vision model described it → agent reasoned about page content and responded with a text summary
- Evidence after fix: Screen recording showing the full flow: agent takes screenshot, vision model processes it, agent returns a coherent text description of the page content. See "Video: After" above.
- Observed result after fix: Tool result contains `[analyzed by bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro]` attribution + wrapped text description. Main model (hy3-preview) successfully reads and reasons about the Discord channel content.
- What was not tested: Remote/node browser proxy screenshots; multiple fallback candidates in sequence (only single-candidate tested live); per-agent auth profile selection.
- Fresh ClawSweeper-flagged proof at the current HEAD: scenario (b) "vision-failure fallback with `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` configured — confirming the returned image block is downscaled by the sanitization guard" is captured by the **Video: Fallback sanitization** above plus the cited `[agents/tool-images] Image resized to fit limits: 2000x1140px 74.8KB -> 9.2KB` gateway log line (the raw `image` content block itself is not visible in webchat UI, hence the log-based supplement). Scenario (a) "directive-shaped `MEDIA:/tmp/secret.png` in page or vision output does not synthesize media delivery" is covered by the unit + integration regression tests landed in this PR (`extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts` and `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts`).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Text-only main model + browser screenshot with `browser.models` configured → text description returned, agent reasons about page
  - Vision call failure → fallback to image block + warning text
  - No `browser.models` configured → original image block behavior unchanged
  - Vision text containing `MEDIA:` directive line → neutralized in tool-result text, `details.media` absent (regression test)
  - Vision failure fallback with `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` → fallback `imageResultFromFile` receives matching `imageSanitization` (regression test)
- Edge cases checked:
  - Local file path passed as `mediaUrl` — fixed: only forward HTTP(S) URLs to describeImageFileWithModel
  - Provider mismatch (`agent-plan` vs `bytedance`) — learned that provider id must match the media-understanding registry, same as `tools.media.image`
  - `wrapExternalContent` applied to vision output — verified in code review
  - `maxBytes` file stat guard — verified via unit test
  - `profile`/`preferredProfile` forwarding — verified end-to-end through runtime-types → runtime.ts → describeImage
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Remote/node browser proxy screenshots (only tested host browser)
  - Multiple fallback candidates in sequence (only single-candidate config tested live; unit test covers this path)
  - Per-agent auth profile selection (see Known Limitations)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

### ClawSweeper findings addressed

Previous review round:

- **[P1] Do not advertise vision screenshots as deliverable media** — Fixed: the vision-success path no longer includes any `MEDIA:` directive or `details.media` in the tool result. Raw screenshots are not auto-delivered to chat channels. Related: #44759.
- **[P2] Remove the unused schema import** — Fixed: removed orphaned `ToolsMediaUnderstandingSchema` import from `zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`.

Latest review round:

- **[P1] Neutralize media directives in vision text** (`extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts:801-807`) — Fixed: introduced `neutralizeMediaDirectives` in `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.ts`. The vision-success path now runs `described.text.trim()` through this helper *before* `wrapExternalContent`, inserting `[neutralized] ` in front of every line whose `trimStart()` begins with `MEDIA:` (case-insensitive). `wrapExternalContent` itself does not strip line-start directives and the agent media extractor (`splitMediaFromOutput`) scans every browser tool-result text block; the `browser` tool is on the trusted-media allowlist, so this guard is required to prevent untrusted page or vision-provider output (e.g. `MEDIA:/tmp/secret.png`) from synthesizing channel-deliverable media artifacts. Regression covered:
  - Unit tests in `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts` for the helper itself (no-op fast path, mid-line `MEDIA:` is not defanged, line-start with arbitrary leading whitespace is defanged, case-insensitive, multiple directives in one blob).
  - Integration test in `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` (`neutralizes MEDIA: directives in vision text and does not attach media`) that drives the full `screenshot` tool execution with a vision-provider response containing `MEDIA:/tmp/secret.png` and asserts: no text-content line in the result matches `/^\s*MEDIA:/i`, the secret path text is still preserved verbatim (defanged not deleted), `details.media` is absent, and `imageResultFromFile` is never called on the success path.

- **[P2] Keep image sanitization on vision failure fallback** (`extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts:828-833`) — Fixed: the vision-failure catch block now passes `imageSanitization: resolveRuntimeImageSanitization()` to `imageResultFromFile`, matching what the non-vision screenshot path at the same callsite already supplies. Without this, any provider error (timeout, auth failure, oversized file) silently bypasses the existing `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` guard added in `d5cc0d53b7e3`. Regression covered:
  - Integration test in `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` (`preserves screenshot image sanitization on vision failure fallback`) that configures `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx: 1600`, forces `describeImageFileWithModel` to reject, and asserts the fallback `imageResultFromFile` call receives `imageSanitization: { maxDimensionPx: 1600 }` along with the failure `extraText`.



## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — no behavior change without explicit `browser.models` config.
- Config/env changes? Yes — new optional fields on the top-level `browser` config namespace (`models`, `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, `visionMaxChars`, `visionMaxBytes`, `visionTimeoutSeconds`).
- Migration needed? No — purely additive.
- `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` gains two optional fields (`profile`, `preferredProfile`) — backward compatible, no existing callers affected.

## Known Limitations

- **scope/attachments fields not implemented**: The `tools.media.image` config shape includes `scope` and `attachments` fields, but these are not exposed for `browser` because the browser tool's vision is always triggered on the just-captured screenshot — there is no attachment selection or scope gating semantic. These can be added in a follow-up if a use case emerges.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Vision API latency adds to screenshot tool response time (typically 5–25s depending on provider).
  - Mitigation: Configurable `timeoutSeconds`; on failure, falls back to returning the raw image block so the agent loop is never blocked.
- Risk: Operator configures wrong provider id (e.g. `agent-plan` instead of `bytedance`) and gets silent failures.
  - Mitigation: Error message surfaces the provider/model that failed; the config shape mirrors `tools.media.image` so operators can copy working config directly.
- Risk: Oversized screenshots sent to vision providers.
  - Mitigation: File size checked against `maxBytes` (default 10 MB) before vision call; oversized files skip the candidate with a logged warning.
- Risk: Web page content in vision descriptions could contain prompt injection.
  - Mitigation: Vision output wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` with warning, matching existing snapshot/tabs treatment.
